title: Finite State Machines
summary: Moore and Mealy machines — the control brains of digital systems.

sections.heading: What Is an FSM?
sections.body: A finite state machine (FSM) is a sequential model with a finite set of states, transitions triggered by inputs, and outputs. FSMs describe controllers: UART TX/RX, traffic lights, CPU microcode, bus arbiters, and protocol handshakes.
bullets: Next-state logic: combinational function of state + inputs
Output logic: Moore (state only) or Mealy (state + inputs)

sections.heading: Moore vs Mealy
sections.body: Moore machines output depends only on current state — outputs are registered and glitch-free during state residence. Mealy machines output depends on state AND inputs — can react one cycle faster but outputs may glitch when inputs change.
bullets: Mealy often needs fewer states for same behavior
Moore outputs align cleanly with clock cycles

callout.text: Default to Moore for clean registered outputs; use Mealy when minimizing state count or latency.

sections.heading: State Encoding
sections.body: States can be encoded as binary, one-hot, or Gray code. One-hot (one flip-flop per state) simplifies next-state logic in FPGAs. Binary encoding minimizes flip-flop count for large FSMs.
bullets: Binary: log₂(N) flip-flops for N states
One-hot: N flip-flops, fast in FPGA LUTs
Gray: adjacent states differ by one bit

sections.heading: FSMs in Verilog
sections.body: The two-process style separates state register (always_ff) from next-state and output logic (always_comb). Use enumerated types or localparams for readable state names. Full case and default prevent latches.
bullets: typedef enum logic [1:0] { IDLE, BUSY, DONE } state_t;
always_comb: case(state) ... endcase for next_state and outputs

sections.heading: FSM Design Steps
sections.body: 1) Draw state diagram with all transitions labeled. 2) Build state table. 3) Encode states. 4) Derive next-state and output equations. 5) Code in RTL. 6) Verify with directed and random simulation.

quiz.question: Moore machine outputs depend on:
quiz.explanation: Moore outputs are functions of present state.

quiz.question: Mealy machine can have fewer states because:
options: Outputs encode input-dependent behavior without extra states
quiz.explanation: Input-dependent outputs in Mealy reduce need for separate wait states.

quiz.question: One-hot encoding for 5 states uses:
quiz.explanation: One-hot: exactly one bit high — one FF per state.

quiz.question: In two-process FSM style, state register uses:
quiz.explanation: State storage is sequential — always_ff @(posedge clk).

quiz.question: Unreachable states in an FSM should:
quiz.explanation: Default case routes illegal states to reset/idle for safe recovery.

quiz.question: A traffic light controller is typically modeled as:
quiz.explanation: Sequential states (red, yellow, green) with timed transitions = FSM.

flashcards.back: Mealy can react same cycle as input; Moore waits until next state
FF block for state + comb block for next state/outputs
Merging equivalent states to reduce flip-flop count
Outputs change when inputs change, even in same state
